Man in Red Tie.description: Wearing a dark suit and red tie, speaking on Fox Business, identified as 'BEN STEIN | 'THE CAPITALIST CODE' AUTHOR', with a calm, authoritative male voice stating, 'Investors who have been riding the wave...'

Man in Blue Shirt.description: Smiling, wearing a blue button-up shirt under a dark jacket, standing in a bright, homey living room with a fireplace and bookshelves.

His voice is heard asserting, 'And the skyrocketing stock market, and that benefits everyone.'

raw_segments: The video opens with a black screen, then the red Netflix logo appears on a gray background, accompanied by a distinct, ethereal sound effect and a brief, suspenseful musical sting. The scene quickly shifts to a man in a blue shirt smiling in a cozy living room, then cuts to a news studio where a woman in a white blazer speaks in front of a 'MONEY with Melissa Francis' backdrop. This is followed by a man in a suit on a green screen labeled 'THE ECONOMY'. Over these visuals, an exasperated male voice questions, "I've always wondered myself, why do we have to hear this every night? What the stock market is doing." Historical newspaper clippings highlighting stock market records from 1976 and 2013 then appear, while a rapid succession of excited male news anchor voices declare "70 record closing highs so far for the...", "blasting through a ceiling", and "in a record-setting IPO". A vintage black car drives through a suburban neighborhood, followed by a modern news studio with three people discussing the Dow closing near 26,000. A man in a red tie, identified as Ben Stein on Fox Business, is shown speaking, with his calm, authoritative voice stating, "Investors who have been riding the wave..." A clear female voice then begins to narrate, explaining, "When the stock market is booming, we're made to believe the economy is booming." President Donald Trump delivers a speech at a podium, with Vice President Mike Pence and another man seated behind him, as the female voice continues, "And in America, the stock market has been mostly booming for almost 40 years." The scene transitions to a black-and-white shot of a man and woman in a 1950s-style kitchen, followed by a graph showing the US GDP growth rate from 1960 to 2020. Over the GDP graph, a formal male voice states, "But the stock market goes, so goes the wealth and the health of the American economy." The final frame displays a graph of US average hourly wages from 1985 to 2015. A confident male voice declares, "What the market is telling us is that we are on the road to prosperity," followed by President Trump's voice asserting, "And the skyrocketing stock market, and that benefits everyone." Another male voice adds, "The stock market has gained almost three trillion dollars in value since the election." The female narrator then returns, providing a counterpoint: "But if you add up all the goods and services bought and sold in the United States, the actual economy, that number isn't growing as quickly as it used to. Wages have hardly budged in decades, and the average American..." before fading out.

event_blocks.summary: The narrative begins by questioning the stock market's reflection of family net worth, which has struggled to recover from the Great Recession, contrasting this with the historical view of the stock exchange as a barometer of American prosperity, evidenced by past market highs and discussions of a 'new kind of gold rush.' News segments highlight U.S. market performance with financial tickers and 'BREAKING NEWS' about dividend stocks, alongside phrases like 'global economic expansion' and 'historic territory,' all while a bustling trading floor is shown. A sudden shift occurs with 'BREAKING NEWS: STOCKS PLUNGING AFTER FAILURE OF BAILOUT BILL IN THE HOUSE,' as an older man in a split-screen reacts gravely to commentary describing a 'fundamentally psychopathic philosophy,' leading to the distinct sound of a stock market bell being rung. To explain what the stock market measures, the scene transitions to a bright, playful setting where a young girl, Jill, running a lemonade stand with an 'EXPANSION PLAN' banner, expresses frustration with banks denying her loan requests. The narrator explains Jill's option to 'go public' through an Initial Public Offering (IPO), symbolized by Jill signing a mock 'Lemonade Stand Corporation' stock certificate. In a subsequent scene, Jill sells shares to Sam, the Boy in Blue Shirt, who places the certificate next to lemonade and money, representing the IPO. Jill enthusiastically declares her intention to grow her 'lemonade empire' by using the funds for new stands and products, visually reinforced by the 'EXPANSION PLAN' banner. Jill then distributes 'DIVIDEND' envelopes to other children, illustrating profit sharing, which the narrator notes excites investors like Sam. Sam, holding certificates, confidently states Jill is 'the smartest girl' and that her business 'is gonna be huge,' anticipating future stock appreciation as he believes he can 'sell these shares for even more later on,' all presented in a clean, brightly lit studio with simple props and upbeat music.

She is identified as 'Jill' by the narrator and herself, stating with enthusiasm, 'And I grow my lemonade empire!'

Wolff, New York University, visually accompanying a female narrator's question about why 'families' net worth still hasn't recovered from the Great Recession.'

Identified as 'Sam' by the narrator, he confidently declares, 'Jill is the smartest girl in the whole world, and I know this lemonade stand thing is gonna be huge,' and later, 'If Jill keeps this up, I can sell these shares for even more later on,' emphasizing his belief in stock appreciation.

raw_segments: The video opens with a bar graph titled 'US AVERAGE FAMILY NET WORTH' displaying fluctuations from 1992 to 2016, with percentages indicating growth or decline, sourced from E. Wolff, New York University, as a female narrator questions why "families' net worth still hasn't recovered from the Great Recession," asking, "So, what exactly is the stock market measuring?" A cash register sound accompanies the transition to a modern stock exchange interior, where a large digital ticker displays stock prices with a yellow circle highlighting a specific data point, while a male voice proclaims, "The barometer of America's prosperity has been the stock exchange." A black-and-white historical crowd scene, overlaid with 'A Netflix original documentary series,' is accompanied by a flurry of excited news clips and voices discussing market highs: "Look at the Dow, currently up by 100," "The NASDAQ finally hit 5,000," and questions like "Is this a new kind of gold rush?" The scene cuts to a news studio where a female anchor in a green blazer reports on U.S. markets with financial tickers below, followed by a male anchor in a suit on a 'MAD MONEY' set with a 'BREAKING NEWS' banner about dividend stocks. The rapid-fire audio continues with phrases like "global economic expansion" and "historic territory." The video then shows a bustling trading floor with traders and a large screen displaying stock data, overlaid with 'A Vox production.' A female CNBC anchor's voice cuts through, reporting "BREAKING NEWS: STOCKS PLUNGING AFTER FAILURE OF BAILOUT BILL IN THE HOUSE," as her image appears on screen. An older man in a suit is seen in a split-screen, reacting with a serious expression to market news, while a male voice describes a "fundamentally psychopathic philosophy." This is followed by a close-up of a large bell being rung, accompanied by the distinct sound of the bell and the text 'The stock market, explained.' The scene transitions to a bright, playful setting, underscored by upbeat, slightly whimsical background music with a gentle rhythm. A female narrator explains, "To understand what the stock market's measuring, it helps to imagine a very simple business. Like a lemonade stand." The scene shows a young girl in a yellow shirt, identified as "Jill" by the narrator, running a lemonade stand with a 'LEMONADE' sign and an 'EXPANSION PLAN' banner. Jill's voice is heard expressing her ambition to think "bigger" and her frustration with banks, stating, "But I'm thinking bigger! I tried to get a loan, but the banks said it was too risky. Rich investors weren't buying." She interacts with another girl in a green shirt wearing a top hat, labeled 'BANK.' The narrator then states, "Jill has another option. She can go public." The scene ends with Jill holding and signing a mock stock certificate for 'Lemonade Stand Corporation,' symbolizing an IPO, with the Netflix logo in the corner.

The video opens with an upbeat, playful background music as a child in a green shirt hands a dollar bill to another child, setting a playful, educational financial narrative. A female narrator explains this is about "giving anyone who wants to, the chance to invest in her business through something called an initial public offering, or IPO." The scene quickly shifts to a Young Girl in Yellow Shirt, identified as Jill, holding a hand-drawn 'Lemonade Stand Corporation' stock certificate, which the narrator clarifies means investors pay "a certain amount, say a dollar, to own a small part, or share of Jill's business." Jill then hands the certificate to a Boy in Blue Shirt, identified as Sam, who places it on a table next to a pitcher of lemonade and a stack of money, symbolizing the IPO. The narrator continues, explaining that "Jill sells a bunch of shares," and Jill herself enthusiastically declares, "And I grow my lemonade empire!" The narrator elaborates that Jill can put that money towards "opening new lemonade stands," which means "more profits," or "developing new products." Above the Lemonade Stand, a hand-drawn 'EXPANSION PLAN' banner with illustrations of a house and trees hangs, visually representing these growth strategies. Jill, acting as the business owner, counts her earnings and then distributes money to other children, who receive envelopes labeled 'DIVIDEND,' illustrating profit sharing, which the narrator explains are "called dividends." The narrator adds that while Jill "doesn't have to do this," it "does help get people excited about her company and more likely to buy her stock, like Sam." The Boy in the Blue Shirt, Sam, now holding a stack of certificates, smiles confidently as text overlays appear, declaring 'Jill is the SMARTEST girl' and 'Lemonade Stand thing is gonna BE HUG.' Sam's voice confirms this, stating, "Jill is the smartest girl in the whole world, and I know this lemonade stand thing is gonna be huge." He then contemplates the future, with the text 'I can sell these shares for even MORE later on,' and his voice reiterates, "If Jill keeps this up, I can sell these shares for even more later on," emphasizing the concept of stock appreciation. The entire sequence unfolds in a clean, brightly lit studio with a neutral background, using simple props, hand-drawn signs, and expressive gestures to teach financial concepts like IPOs, dividends, and stock growth in a child-friendly manner, all underscored by the continuous upbeat music.

event_blocks.summary: A boy in a blue shirt holds a lemonade stand stock certificate and a dollar bill, followed by a girl in a yellow shirt counting money, as a narrator explains the stock market involves buying and selling company pieces based on future worth. A stylized world map with global pins illustrates this happening thousands of times per second worldwide, with the New York Stock Exchange (NYSE) identified as the primary market. Historical animation depicts 18th-century figures under a buttonwood tree on Wall Street, marking the NYSE's founding in 1792, followed by a building transforming into a skyscraper and the NYSE logo appearing alongside corporate logos like IBM and GE. A hand-drawn computer displays the Nasdaq logo and the year 1971, introduced as the electronic, location-less 'cooler, younger brother' where tech companies like Apple and Facebook are traded. The video concludes with NYSE and Nasdaq logos side-by-side, labeled 'STOCK MARKET', emphasizing their combined importance for gauging the market's performance, and an 'INDEX' machine graphic clarifies how indices consolidate numerous share prices into a single number.

Corporate Logos Montage.description: A sequence of major company logos (IBM, ExxonMobil, P&G, GE, Walmart) appearing around the NYSE logo, illustrating the diversity of companies listed on the stock exchange, specifically mentioning 'big traditional companies like IBM and GE' as being traded there.

World Map.description: Hand-drawn outline of the world with black pins marking various global locations, symbolizing the global reach of the stock market, where trading is described as 'happening thousands of times a second, all over the world'.

raw_segments: The video opens with a close-up of a young boy in a blue shirt, holding a lemonade stand stock certificate and a dollar bill, symbolizing investment and profit. This transitions to a girl in a yellow shirt, also holding the certificate and counting money, reinforcing the theme of entrepreneurship, as a female narrator states, "That's the stock market." The narrator explains, "It's people buying and selling tiny pieces of companies based on how much they think those pieces will be worth in the future." The scene then transitions to a stylized, hand-drawn world map with pins marking global locations, visually representing the narrator's point that "in real life, it's happening thousands of times a second, all over the world." The narrator continues, "There are stock markets everywhere, but the New York Stock Exchange is the big kahuna." A black-and-white historical animation then depicts 18th-century figures in formal attire, with the text 'FOUNDED 1792' appearing, as the narrator explains its origin: "It's been around since 1792, when 24 stockbrokers put on their finest short pants and top hats and got together under a buttonwood tree on Wall Street in New York City." The animation continues with a building transforming into a modern skyscraper, symbolizing growth. The NYSE logo appears, followed by a montage of major corporations like IBM, ExxonMobil, P&G, GE, and Walmart, illustrating the evolution of the stock market, with the narrator specifying, "Today, it's where shares in big traditional companies like IBM and GE are traded." The scene transitions to a hand-drawn computer displaying the Nasdaq logo, with the year '1971' appearing, marking its founding. The narrator introduces it as "the cooler, younger brother," explaining it "was born in 1971 and doesn't have a physical location. All the trading happens electronically." The computer is then shown with logos of Apple and Facebook, highlighting modern tech companies, as the narrator confirms, "That's where you find tech companies like Apple and Facebook." The video concludes with a graphic of the NYSE and Nasdaq logos side by side, labeled 'STOCK MARKET', as the narrator explains their combined importance: "So, in America, if you want to know how the stock market is doing, you want to know how both these exchanges are doing." This is followed by a mechanical machine labeled 'INDEX', representing the structure and function of stock market indices, with the narrator clarifying, "That's where indexes come in. They take a whole bunch of share prices and transform them into one clean number."

event_blocks.summary: The S&P 500 INDEX, tracking 500 large companies, and the more exclusive DOW JONES INDUSTRIAL AVERAGE, following 30 companies like Apple after its 2015 replacement of AT&T, are visually represented by a stylized 'Index Machine' processing rapidly increasing numbers, while a female narrator explains their significance as major American indexes. A hand-drawn world map with pins on 'FRANKFURT' and 'SHANGHAI' transitions to real-world footage of traders and stock tickers, accompanied by rapid-fire news reports from multiple male voices announcing global market movements, including record highs for the German DAX, movements in London's FTSE 100, and drops in the Nikkei and Shanghai indexes. The female narrator notes that while many large companies are publicly traded now, this wasn't always the case, leading into a split-screen showing a historical animation of a crowned man with a globe and a political cartoon of two men on a tightrope. Lynn Stout, a Corporate Law Professor, explains from a grand, wood-paneled hall that historically, 'one guy, and it was almost always a guy,' like Andrew Carnegie, Cornelius Vanderbilt, and John D. Rockefeller, exercised tight control over 'big corporations of the 1900s' as single shareholders, with accompanying historical footage and sounds of steel mills, trains, and oil derricks. Stout later elaborates on the rise of corporations in the early 20th century, illustrated by 'GM DIESEL' and 'GENERAL ELECTRIC' signs, emphasizing that public share ownership enables faster growth and that shareholders' profit desire drives good decisions, visually supported by a children's business simulation where kids write 'IPO' on a whiteboard, celebrate with 'EXPANSION PLAN' and 'NEW LEMONADE POPSICLES' signs, and engage in financial transactions, including a girl holding a 'Lemonade Stand Corporation' stock certificate and counting money, while another acts as a banker. Stout highlights the stock market as a force for good, creating jobs, and concludes that by the mid-20th century, the American public corporation proved beneficial, reinforced by historical footage of factory workers and construction sites, with the Netflix logo visible throughout. A vintage scene of a man on a lawnmower is followed by footage of a man working in a kitchen and a woman with a 'TUPPERWARE' sign, as Stout explains the stock market's role in post-WWII American prosperity. Two men in suits examine a document before a classic male narrator declares a 'new era' advocating for democracy and capital flow. Stout, in a modern hall, explains the corporation's intended role in providing investment opportunities to average Americans for superior returns, punctuated by a theatrical female voice asking, 'Don't you want to be an investor, honey?' Stout continues, stressing job creation and innovation, illustrated by a stylized 'stakeholder' diagram showing 'shareholders,' 'bondholders,' 'suppliers,' and 'employees,' as she articulates that executives viewed themselves as stewards serving all stakeholders and the community, reinforced by historical factory and construction site scenes, with the classic male narrator mentioning Buick. The Netflix logo is consistently in the top right corner.

raw_segments: The segment opens with a stylized, hand-drawn 'Index Machine' labeled 'S&P 500 INDEX,' visually processing a dollar sign and rapidly increasing numbers from '2,000' to '20,000.' A female narrator explains that the S&P 500 tracks 500 of the largest companies, while the machine switches to 'DOW JONES INDUSTRIAL AVERAGE,' which she notes is more exclusive, following only 30 companies. She details how AT&T was replaced by Apple in the Dow in 2015, emphasizing these are big American indexes. The scene transitions to a hand-drawn world map with pins marking global financial centers like 'FRANKFURT' and 'SHANGHAI,' accompanied by real-world footage of traders and stock tickers. Rapid-fire news reports from multiple male voices announce global market movements, including "The German stock index DAX reached another record high," "London's FTSE 100 index," "The Nikkei index closed below," and "The Shanghai index dropped." event_blocks.summary: Robert Shiller, in a living room with a fireplace, explained that stock prices are driven by popular beliefs about companies rather than their real value, a concept illustrated by an animated graph contrasting 'Real Value' with 'Stories.' This narrative was exemplified by Chipotle's stock plunge, attributed by a male news anchor to outbreaks like E. coli, and Volkswagen's stock freefall due to an emission scandal, as reported by another male anchor. The female narrator observed that sometimes these stories are mere hype, a sentiment underscored by a chaotic trading floor scene where a news anchor declared internet companies the hottest investments, citing Lycos, Excite, and Yahoo! A vintage laptop showed Lycos's stock soaring from $13 to $107 and then $36 to $207, with a news anchor confirming their meteoric rise. A 1999 NBC news anchor's appearance coincided with Robert Shiller's return, where he reiterated the 1990s narrative of internet companies dominating and not needing to focus on profits, reinforcing his commentary on market narratives versus real value, all while the Netflix logo remained visible in the top right corner.

event_blocks.summary: Archival audio captures a woman expressing discouragement with rising prices, leading into a New York Times clipping about a sharp stock market decline in 1972, which Lynn Stout, a Corporate Law Professor, contextualizes with a "general sense of concern" about corporate operations. Milton Friedman, introduced as the "chief finger-pointer," is shown on The Phil Donahue Show in 1979, debating the role of greed in capitalism and advocating for a shareholder-centric corporate model, as illustrated by an animated stakeholder diagram and a New York Times op-ed titled 'A Friedman doctrine—The Social Responsibility Of Business Is to Increase Its Profits.' Stout elaborates that Friedman argued corporations, owned by shareholders, had the sole obligation to increase profits, a philosophy that influenced corporate behavior, leading to executive pay being tied to share price performance, as depicted by a stylized animation of CEO pay versus stock price. This focus on short-term stock price increases, through cost-cutting or stock buybacks, is contrasted with the needs of employees, customers, and the environment. The narrative then shifts to the financial allocation of corporate earnings between 2007-2016, showing 55% on stock buybacks and 39% on dividends, leaving little for wages or development, a point reinforced by Hank Newell, former CEO of Wausau Paper, who emphasizes a long-term company view over short-term returns. The conflict between a hedge fund pushing for dividend increases and Wausau Paper's management, who prioritized investment in tissue paper production, is highlighted through newspaper clippings and an animated flow of funds. Jeff Weisenberger, a former machinist, expresses frustration over employee concessions and pay cuts. The closure of the Wausau Paper Brokaw mill, impacting 450 workers, is reported by FOX 6 NEWS, with Weisenberger recounting the emotional impact of the layoffs. Newell voices concern over the shift towards shorter-term shareholder rights over longer-term stakeholder responsibilities, a trend Stout notes is "seriously threatening" corporate sustainability. The segment concludes with visuals of a dilapidated building and a BLOCKIT MOLD REPAIR truck. A rapid montage of news anchors celebrates the stock market's rise, with animated graphs illustrating that while the S&P 500 Index surged from 1970-2016, CEO compensation spiked to 271 times the average worker's pay by 2016, up from 22 times in 1973. A graph shows fewer Americans invested in the stock market by 2016, reaching a 20-year low, while another dual-axis graph contrasts the rising S&P 500 Index with a fluctuating unemployment rate from 1947-2013.

raw_segments: The segment opens with a close-up of a woman in a black blazer, later identified as Lynn Stout, speaking with a serious expression, as archival audio features a male voice asking, "Are you mad about the way prices have risen?" and a female voice responding, "I'm really discouraged and disgusted with the whole thing." This transitions to a black-and-white New York Times clipping from June 16, 1972, headlining 'Stocks Decline Sharply; Dow Tumbles 14.94.' Lynn Stout, a Corporate Law Professor, then appears in a grand, wood-paneled hall, explaining that there was a "general sense of concern that something had gone wrong in the American economy," and that "the finger got pointed at the way our large public corporations were operating and being run." The scene shifts to archival footage of Milton Friedman, an older man in a light suit, on The Phil Donahue Show in 1979, as a narrator introduces him as "the chief finger-pointer, Milton Friedman," an economist famous enough to be invited on talk shows. On the show, the host, a man with white hair, directly questions Friedman, asking if he ever doubted capitalism or if "greed's a good idea to run on," to which Friedman challenges back, "Tell me, is there some society you know that doesn't run on greed?" An animated stakeholder diagram then appears, showing a blue circle with yellow spokes for 'shareholders,' 'bondholders,' 'suppliers,' 'employees,' and 'community,' as the narrator explains that Friedman "was not a fan" of this concept, believing it "should have exactly one spoke: shareholders." A black-and-white newspaper article titled 'A Friedman doctrine—The Social Responsibility Of Business Is to Increase Its Profits' by Milton Friedman is displayed, with a caption referencing General Motors, as the narrator mentions that in 1970, Friedman "published a blockbuster op-ed," "the famous editorial that ran in the New York Times." Lynn Stout reappears in the grand hall, elaborating on Friedman's op-ed, stating he argued that "because corporations were owned by their shareholders, the only obligation of business was to make profits." The segment concludes with a black-and-white archival clip from 'WALL STREET, 1987,' showing a man intently watching a computer monitor, as the narrator begins to reference "Gordon Gekko's character in the movie..." before the audio cuts off.

The segment opens in a formal boardroom where a male narrator's voice discusses Wall Street epitomizing Friedman's philosophy, as a man in a dark suit and tie speaks into a microphone addressing a panel including 'CHRISTOPHER HAYES' and 'MARGARET HAYES.' A dramatic male voice then declares, "You own the company. That's right. You, the stockholder," continuing to assert that stockholders are "royally screwed over by these bureaucrats," and that "greed, for lack of a better word, is good." The scene transitions to a wide shot of a large, ornate hall with an audience and a '50 YEARS' banner, followed by a stylized animation showing a man in a suit with a yellow tie next to a bar graph comparing 'CEO PAY' and 'STOCK PRICE.' Lynn Stout, a CORPORATE LAW PROFESSOR, then appears in a live interview in a grand, wood-paneled hall, her serious and thoughtful voice explaining that "corporations took his advice," starting to tie "the top executive's pay to share price performance." She elaborates that if "80% of the CEO's pay is based on what the share price is going to do next year, he or she is going to do their best to make sure that share price goes up," even if the consequences are "harmful to employees, to customers, to society, to the environment, or even to the corporation itself in the long term." As she continues to speak, explaining how "CEOs put more money towards things that would increase the stock prices in the short term, like cutting costs, or buying back a bunch of their own shares to decrease the supply and artificially bump up the price," the video shifts to a playful lemonade stand scene with a young girl holding a 'Lemonade Stand Corporation' stock certificate, then concludes with a data visualization titled 'HOW COMPANIES SPENT EARNINGS 2007-2016,' highlighting 55% attributed to W. LAZONICK. The Netflix logo remains consistently in the top right corner throughout.

LAZONICK, UNIVERSITY OF MASSACHUSETTS, visually breaking down corporate spending: 55% on stock buybacks (yellow), 39% on dividends (blue), and 6% on 'EVERYTHING ELSE' (gray), with icons of a dollar sign, building, and beaker illustrating the latter. A female narrator explains that this allocation, particularly the 55% on stock buybacks and 39% on dividends, meant little was left to raise wages, expand, or develop new products, which are crucial for long-term economic health. The scene transitions to Hank Newell, former CEO of Wausau Paper, seated in a well-lit living room, speaking thoughtfully with hand gestures in a calm, measured male voice, emphasizing the importance of a long-term view for a company, contrasting it with the desire for short-term returns within 24 months. Industrial footage of a paper mill with smokestacks and piles of raw material then appears, as the narrator explains that in 2012, Wausau Paper was investing to switch from printing to tissue paper production. This is followed by a close-up of two stacks of paper labeled 'Wausau Paper' and 'New York hedge fund,' symbolizing corporate conflict, and a newspaper clipping from the 'MILWAUKEE WISCONSIN JOURNAL SENTINEL' dated January 17, 2014, with the headline 'New York hedge fund renews criticism of Wausau Paper,' as the narrator details how a hedge fund bought shares and pushed the company to cut costs instead. Hank Newell's voice returns, stating that the hedge fund's argument was to increase the dividend, a move his management team disagreed with. An animated sequence then shows dollar bills flowing from 'PAYROLL,' 'DIVIDENDS,' and 'NEW PRODUCTS' into a 'PROFITS' box, emphasizing financial allocation. The final shot features Jeff Weisenberger, a former machinist at Wausau Paper, seated in a home environment, speaking directly to the camera with a direct, slightly frustrated male voice, mentioning that employees "offered concessions" and "take a cut in pay." The Netflix logo remains consistently in the top right corner throughout.

The video opens with industrial footage of a paper mill, showing large yellow machinery and conveyor belts, while a male news anchor voice reports that "Wausau Paper says it plans to close the Brokaw mill by March 31st," with on-screen text from 'FOX 6 NEWS' and 'Courtesy: WausauPaperCorp' confirming the news about 450 workers to be laid off. The scene transitions to a wide shot of a snow-covered river with a bridge, followed by an aerial view of a factory complex featuring a large water tower labeled 'BROKAW' and a 'WISCONSIN PUBLIC RADIO, DEC 6, 2011' date stamp, as the news anchor continues to explain the closure is "devastating" to the community. The narrative then shifts to an interview with Jeff Weisenberger, a former machinist at Wausau Paper, who, seated in a room with framed photos and wearing a black leather jacket, speaks with a somber tone about losing his job on "December 7th," the same day he was "burying my father," describing the next day at work as a "madhouse" with people "crying." Next, Hank Newell, former CEO of Wausau Paper, appears in a bright living room with a bookshelf, wearing a light blue button-up shirt and glasses, speaking with expressive hand gestures and an articulate voice about his concern that "we've evolved to this much shorter-term view on shareholder rights versus a longer-term view on stakeholder responsibilities." The scene changes to Lynn Stout, a corporate law professor, seated in a grand, wood-paneled hall with chandeliers, wearing a black blazer, speaking directly to the camera with a serious and authoritative demeanor, explaining that a trend has "gotten even more powerful and important" and is "seriously threatening the ability of our corporations to..." before the audio cuts off. The video concludes with exterior shots of a dilapidated building with boarded-up windows and a truck labeled 'Kennedy-Can BLOCKIT MOLD REPAIR', visually reinforcing the theme of economic decline and industrial decay as the audio fades.

The segment opens with Lynn Stout, a Corporate Law Professor, in a grand, wood-paneled hall, speaking directly to the camera with a serious, articulate tone about the importance of long-term corporate sustainability and economic growth, contrasting it with short-term profits that the stock market prioritizes, even if it means laying off workers or closing factories. This is followed by a quick visual sequence, accompanied by a serious musical score, showing a crowded industrial parking lot, a quiet snowy suburban street, and an industrial paper mill representing Wausau Paper. A rapid montage of excited male news anchors then declares the stock market's impressive start, new records, and the S&P 500 reaching an all-time high. As animated graphs appear, Lynn Stout's voice returns, explaining that as the stock market grew, so did CEO paychecks; a yellow line graph of the S&P 500 Index from 1970 to 2016 shows a sharp rise after 1983, while a black line graph of CEO compensation highlights a spike to 271% around 2004, with her noting that CEOs made 271 times more than the average worker by 2016, up from 22 times in 1973. A graph titled 'AMERICANS INVESTED IN THE STOCK MARKET' then illustrates a decline from a 2007 peak of 65% to 52% by 2016, as she states fewer Americans have benefited and the middle class has dropped out, marking a 20-year low for stock market investment. The segment concludes with a dual-axis graph showing the S&P 500 Index rising sharply against a fluctuating unemployment rate from 1947 to 2013, with all graphs featuring a minimalist design, sourced from EPI, Gallup, and NBER, and the Netflix logo consistently present.

event_blocks.summary: A dual-axis line graph from 1947 to 2013 illustrates the rising trends of both the S&P 500 Index and the Gini Coefficient, with a female narrator observing that as stock prices have increased, so has inequality, but asserting that this is not inevitable. The scene shifts to a dynamic stock exchange floor where a man in a suit waves and a trader in a red shirt gestures excitedly, while the narrator explains that stock markets allow people to choose which companies and ideas deserve investment. Economist Robert Shiller, in a blue button-up shirt and dark jacket, speaks in a living room, emphasizing that successful countries have stock markets and that those that previously banned them are now adopting them. The video then shows a modern trading floor before focusing on Corporate Law Professor Lynn Stout, in a black blazer, addressing an audience in a grand hall. The narrator states that stockholders can influence corporate behavior and whose interests are considered. Lynn Stout then directly expresses that most shareholders prioritize their long-term futures, care about their communities and future generations, and desire companies to profit ethically by benefiting the world rather than harming it, concluding that this is the true desire of most shareholders. The narrative transitions to a playful educational segment at a lemonade stand, where a boy in a yellow shirt holds a 'Lemonade Stand Corporation' stock certificate and a girl in a green shirt with a top hat serves as a banker at a 'BANK' booth, accompanied by upbeat music with a prominent bassline and percussion as the children engage with financial elements, symbolizing entrepreneurship. The segment ends with a black screen displaying the production credit 'Produced by ELIZABETH SCHELLENS' in white text as the music continues.

The scene transitions to a bustling stock exchange floor, where a man in a suit waves enthusiastically, followed by a close-up of a trader in a red shirt gesturing excitedly, while the female narrator continues, "Stock markets give people a chance to decide which companies deserve to succeed, which ideas are worth a gamble." The setting then shifts to a homey living room where Nobel Prize-winning economist Robert Shiller, wearing a blue button-up shirt under a dark jacket, speaks with expressive hand gestures, stating, "There's something about giving people games to play. You look at successful countries and they all have stock markets. And countries that tried to shut them down are coming around and instituting them now." The video cuts to a modern trading floor with traders at desks, then to Corporate Law Professor Lynn Stout, in a black blazer, speaking in a grand hall with ornate railings, as the female narrator explains, "Stockholders can influence how companies behave, whose interests they take into account." Lynn Stout then speaks directly, asserting, "Most of us are thinking about our long-term futures. We care about our neighbors and our children and our grandchildren. We have values and morals and want our companies to make money by doing things that are good for the world and not by harming people and destroying it." She concludes, "That's what most shareholders really want." The narrative then shifts to a playful, educational segment featuring children running a lemonade stand; a boy in a yellow shirt holds a hand-drawn 'Lemonade Stand Corporation' stock certificate, while a girl in a green shirt and top hat acts as a banker at a 'BANK' booth. Upbeat, playful music with a distinct bassline and percussion begins, accompanying the children interacting with money and certificates, symbolizing entrepreneurship and finance. The segment concludes with a black screen displaying the production credit: 'Produced by ELIZABETH SCHELLENS' in white text, as the upbeat music continues.
